texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas separation properties of commercially available polyphenylene oxide (PPO) and Cardo-type polyimide (PI) hollow fiber membranes were investigated by CO2/CH4 and O2/N2 separation experiments. The pure gas permeation and the mixed gas separation experiments indicated that the two hollow fiber membranes were suitable candidates for O2/N2 and CO2/CH4 separations. Average O2/N2 permselectivities of 3.9 and 5.7 for PPO and PI hollow fibers, respectively, average CO2/CH4 permselectivities of 16.4 and 36.0 for PPO and PI hollow fibers, respectively, CO2 permeances of 210 and 110 GPU at 100 psig for PPO and PI hollow fibers, and O2 permeances of 40 GPU and 15 GPU for PPO and PI hollow fibers were achieved. The CO2/CH4 mixture separation was carried out at three different CO2 concentrations and three stage-cuts at 100 psig pressure. Separation factors of up to 21.1 and 7.5 for PI and PPO membranes, respectively, and permeances of up to 83 GPU and 140 GPU for PI and PPO, respectively were obtained.
texts.text: Gas separation properties of commercially available polyphenylene oxide (PPO) and Cardo-type polyimide (PI) hollow fiber membranes were investigated by CO2/CH4 and O2/N2 separation experiments. The pure gas permeation and the mixed gas separation experiments indicated that the two hollow fiber membranes were suitable candidates for O2/N2 and CO2/CH4 separations. Average O2/N2 permselectivities of 3.9 and 5.7 for PPO and PI hollow fibers, respectively, average CO2/CH4 permselectivities of 16.4 and 36.0 for PPO and PI hollow fibers, respectively, CO2 permeances of 210 and 110 GPU at 100 psig for PPO and PI hollow fibers, and O2 permeances of 40 GPU and 15 GPU for PPO and PI hollow fibers were achieved. The CO2/CH4 mixture separation was carried out at three different CO2 concentrations and three stage-cuts at 100 psig pressure. Separation factors of up to 21.1 and 7.5 for PI and PPO membranes, respectively, and permeances of up to 83 GPU and 140 GPU for PI and PPO, respectively were obtained.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Furthermore, it was found that CO2 permeance varied proportional to the ratio of stage-cut to feed concentration and it was observed that when the stage cuts were set equal to the concentrations of CO2 in the feed (vol.%), the obtained separation factors were approximately equal. ©2006 Elsevier B.V. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although the concept of gas separation by membranes is more than 150 years old, the industrial applications of this process remained limited for many decades. While the knowledge of such membrane properties roots back in the investigations by Mitchell and Graham [1,2], the major breakthrough came with the development of high flux asymmetric cellulose acetate (CA)membranesbyLoebandSourirajan[3].Althoughtheearly applications of CA membranes were in water desalination, they exhibited high permeation rates and permselectivities to gases if properly dried [4,5].
texts.text: Although the concept of gas separation by membranes is more than 150 years old, the industrial applications of this process remained limited for many decades. While the knowledge of such membrane properties roots back in the investigations by Mitchell and Graham [1,2], the major breakthrough came with the development of high flux asymmetric cellulose acetate (CA)membranesbyLoebandSourirajan[3].Althoughtheearly applications of CA membranes were in water desalination, they exhibited high permeation rates and permselectivities to gases if properly dried [4,5].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: simplicity, weight and space efficiency, and operation flexibility. Onthe other hand, membranes cannot compete with some of the traditional technologies on the basis of product purity. In many applications a hybrid system consisting of membranes for bulk separation and a secondary process for final purification proves to be the most efficient and economical alternative. For example, in the case of natural gas sweetening, the bulk of CO2 can be removed by membranes while the final purification to pipeline quality can be achieved by an amine absorption process. On the other hand, a capacity increase may result from integration of membrane units to an existing amine plant.
texts.text: simplicity, weight and space efficiency, and operation flexibility. Onthe other hand, membranes cannot compete with some of the traditional technologies on the basis of product purity. In many applications a hybrid system consisting of membranes for bulk separation and a secondary process for final purification proves to be the most efficient and economical alternative. For example, in the case of natural gas sweetening, the bulk of CO2 can be removed by membranes while the final purification to pipeline quality can be achieved by an amine absorption process. On the other hand, a capacity increase may result from integration of membrane units to an existing amine plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Selection of a membrane for gas separation applications is essentially based on its gas permeation properties such as permselectivity, mechanical strength and long-term durability. However, most membranes that show high permeability for gases usually exhibit low to moderate permselectivity. This trade-off was demonstrated by Robeson's upper bound lines [6,7].
texts.text: Selection of a membrane for gas separation applications is essentially based on its gas permeation properties such as permselectivity, mechanical strength and long-term durability. However, most membranes that show high permeability for gases usually exhibit low to moderate permselectivity. This trade-off was demonstrated by Robeson's upper bound lines [6,7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: [8]) possess excellent separation properties, which make them suitable candidates for gas separations. It has been reported that these two polymers perform close to or higher than the Robeson's trade-offline [9,10].
texts.text: [8]) possess excellent separation properties, which make them suitable candidates for gas separations. It has been reported that these two polymers perform close to or higher than the Robeson's trade-offline [9,10].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyimide polymers are known as suitable materials for gas separation membranes with high permselectivities compared to other glassy membranes. For example, the Cardo 1 -type polyimide (PI) hollow fiber membranes developed by 'Research Institute of Innovative Technology for the Earth' (RITE, Japan) for CO2/N2 separation showed higher CO2/N2 permselectivity than commercially available hollow fiber membranes and excellent properties such as high permselectivity and permeability; ease of fabrication (excellent solubility in organic solvents); excellent thermal stability [12]. The high CO2/N2 permselectivity exhibited by PI membranes is suggested to be due to their high CO2 solubility compared to conventional polymers, whereas the gas diffusivity of these polymers is nearly equal to that of the commercial polymers [13]. The same group [12-15] studied the gas separation properties of Cardo-type polyimide hollow fibers including: wet spinning method developed by Karashima et al. [12] to produce sponge-type support structure with inner defectless skin layer, introduction of functional groups with CO2 affinity [14] and improving the mechanical strength of these hollow fibers by blending Cardo-type polyimides and polyamides [12]. However, all these studies only dealt with CO2/N2 separation.
texts.text: Polyimide polymers are known as suitable materials for gas separation membranes with high permselectivities compared to other glassy membranes. For example, the Cardo 1 -type polyimide (PI) hollow fiber membranes developed by 'Research Institute of Innovative Technology for the Earth' (RITE, Japan) for CO2/N2 separation showed higher CO2/N2 permselectivity than commercially available hollow fiber membranes and excellent properties such as high permselectivity and permeability; ease of fabrication (excellent solubility in organic solvents); excellent thermal stability [12]. The high CO2/N2 permselectivity exhibited by PI membranes is suggested to be due to their high CO2 solubility compared to conventional polymers, whereas the gas diffusivity of these polymers is nearly equal to that of the commercial polymers [13]. The same group [12-15] studied the gas separation properties of Cardo-type polyimide hollow fibers including: wet spinning method developed by Karashima et al. [12] to produce sponge-type support structure with inner defectless skin layer, introduction of functional groups with CO2 affinity [14] and improving the mechanical strength of these hollow fibers by blending Cardo-type polyimides and polyamides [12]. However, all these studies only dealt with CO2/N2 separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyphenylene oxide (PPO) is another membrane with industrial gas separation applications. PPO is a glassy polymer with glass transition temperature ( T g) ranging from 212 to 218 ◦ C. Amongst all glassy polymers, PPO shows one of the highest permeabilities to gases. This high permeability is attributed to the absence of polar groups in the main chain of PPO [16]. PPO also possesses excellent mechanical and thermal properties and high impact strength [17]. However, despite favorable properties of high molecular weight PPO [10,18] most of the reported studies use low molecular weight PPO.
texts.text: Polyphenylene oxide (PPO) is another membrane with industrial gas separation applications. PPO is a glassy polymer with glass transition temperature ( T g) ranging from 212 to 218 ◦ C. Amongst all glassy polymers, PPO shows one of the highest permeabilities to gases. This high permeability is attributed to the absence of polar groups in the main chain of PPO [16]. PPO also possesses excellent mechanical and thermal properties and high impact strength [17]. However, despite favorable properties of high molecular weight PPO [10,18] most of the reported studies use low molecular weight PPO.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Furthermore, while industrial application membranes have asymmetric structures, only a limited number of data are available on the gas separation properties of such membranes. For example, all the published data on commercially available Cardo-type polyimide hollow fibers are limited to CO2/N2 separation [11-14]. Similarly, most of the published literature on commercial PPO membranes deals with O2/N2 separation. In the light of the above discussion the present study is an attempt to bridge the above mentioned gaps in the area of industrial membrane gas separation. The objective of this work is to investigate the potentials of commercially available asymmetric PI and PPO membranes in the field of natural gas treatment.
texts.text: Furthermore, while industrial application membranes have asymmetric structures, only a limited number of data are available on the gas separation properties of such membranes. For example, all the published data on commercially available Cardo-type polyimide hollow fibers are limited to CO2/N2 separation [11-14]. Similarly, most of the published literature on commercial PPO membranes deals with O2/N2 separation. In the light of the above discussion the present study is an attempt to bridge the above mentioned gaps in the area of industrial membrane gas separation. The objective of this work is to investigate the potentials of commercially available asymmetric PI and PPO membranes in the field of natural gas treatment.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Materials and modules
texts.text: 2.1. Materials and modules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The commercial PPO hollow fiber samples were generously supplied by Aquilo Gas Separation B.V. (Parker Filtration and Separation B.V., The Netherlands). The inside and outside diameters of the hollow fibers were 370 and 520 𝛍 m,respectively. The PPOmoduleswereassembled by loading a bundle of 10 fibers in a shell and tube structure. The feed gas was introduced inside the fibers and permeate was collected on the shell side. The selective dense layers of the hollow fibers were located at the outside and the permeances of the gases were calculated based on the outer surface area of hollow fibers. The Cardo-type polyimide hollow fiber modules were generously supplied by RITE, Japan. The inside and outside diameters of fibers were 370 and 500 𝛍 m, respectively.
texts.text: The commercial PPO hollow fiber samples were generously supplied by Aquilo Gas Separation B.V. (Parker Filtration and Separation B.V., The Netherlands). The inside and outside diameters of the hollow fibers were 370 and 520 𝛍 m,respectively. The PPOmoduleswereassembled by loading a bundle of 10 fibers in a shell and tube structure. The feed gas was introduced inside the fibers and permeate was collected on the shell side. The selective dense layers of the hollow fibers were located at the outside and the permeances of the gases were calculated based on the outer surface area of hollow fibers. The Cardo-type polyimide hollow fiber modules were generously supplied by RITE, Japan. The inside and outside diameters of fibers were 370 and 500 𝛍 m, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Five membrane modules were named RITE-A, RITE-B, PPO#1, PPO#2, and PPO#3. The first two modules contained PI membranes and the second three modules contained PPO membranes. The experimental works were done using RITE-A and PPO#1 modules. The other three modules, namely, RITE-B, PPO#2, and PPO#3, were used only for reproducibility experiments. Table 1 shows the specifications of these modules.
texts.text: Five membrane modules were named RITE-A, RITE-B, PPO#1, PPO#2, and PPO#3. The first two modules contained PI membranes and the second three modules contained PPO membranes. The experimental works were done using RITE-A and PPO#1 modules. The other three modules, namely, RITE-B, PPO#2, and PPO#3, were used only for reproducibility experiments. Table 1 shows the specifications of these modules.

texts.text: Ultra high purity CO2, CH4, N2, and O2, as well as CO2/CH4 gas mixture (5%, 10% and 24.6% CO2-balance CH4) were purchased from Praxair Canada.

texts.orig: The CO2/CH4 separation experiments were conducted at room temperature (22.5 ◦ C) and 100 psig pressure. The permeates were collected at ambient pressure. The experiments with each of the three CO2/CH4 gas mixtures were conducted at three stage-cuts as described below:
texts.text: The CO2/CH4 separation experiments were conducted at room temperature (22.5 ◦ C) and 100 psig pressure. The permeates were collected at ambient pressure. The experiments with each of the three CO2/CH4 gas mixtures were conducted at three stage-cuts as described below:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The compositions of the retentate and permeate were determined by a gas chromatograph (Varian 3400) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a 'HayeSep Q' column. The compositions were corrected using thermal response factors, TRFs. The TRF values were carefully calculated for each gas following Dietz method [19]. The obtained TRF values were 48 and 36.2 for CO2 and CH4, respectively, which were in good agreement with those calculated by Dietz, namely, 48 and 35.7, respectively. The retentate flow rates were measured using a wet-test-meter and the permeation rates were measured using a bubble flow meter. The stage-cut was controlled by a metering valve at the retentate side.
texts.text: The compositions of the retentate and permeate were determined by a gas chromatograph (Varian 3400) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a 'HayeSep Q' column. The compositions were corrected using thermal response factors, TRFs. The TRF values were carefully calculated for each gas following Dietz method [19]. The obtained TRF values were 48 and 36.2 for CO2 and CH4, respectively, which were in good agreement with those calculated by Dietz, namely, 48 and 35.7, respectively. The retentate flow rates were measured using a wet-test-meter and the permeation rates were measured using a bubble flow meter. The stage-cut was controlled by a metering valve at the retentate side.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Results and discussion
texts.text: 4. Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Pure gas permeation experiments
texts.text: 4.1. Pure gas permeation experiments

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.1. Pure gas permeation using Cardo-type polyimide (PI) hollow fiber membranes
texts.text: 4.1.1. Pure gas permeation using Cardo-type polyimide (PI) hollow fiber membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeances of pure O2 and CO2, and permselectivities of O2/N2 and CO2/CH4 through PI hollow fibers are shown in Fig. 2. Both CO2 and O2 permeances, as well as CO2/CH4 permselectivity increased with feed pressure, whereas O2/N2 permselectivity remained constant within the experimental range. The reproducibility of the above results was examined by repeating some of the experiments using a second PI hollow fiber module (RITE-B). Similar results were obtained within maximum 20% variation.
texts.text: The permeances of pure O2 and CO2, and permselectivities of O2/N2 and CO2/CH4 through PI hollow fibers are shown in Fig. 2. Both CO2 and O2 permeances, as well as CO2/CH4 permselectivity increased with feed pressure, whereas O2/N2 permselectivity remained constant within the experimental range. The reproducibility of the above results was examined by repeating some of the experiments using a second PI hollow fiber module (RITE-B). Similar results were obtained within maximum 20% variation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CH4 and N2 permeation experiments at 100 psig were repeated after the membrane was exposed to CO2 and O2, respectively. The repeat experiments were carried out for a period of 8 h for each gas. N2 permeance after exposure to O2 showed 5% higher value compared to that of before exposure to O2 with the value of 3.28 GPU compared with 3.14 GPU. The permeance of CH4 after CO2 was higher than that before CO2 at the beginning, but gradually declined and leveled off at a slightly lower value as shown in Fig. 3. This observation is explained by a fading plasticization effect of CO2, which allowed the polymer chains resume their original shape and rigidity. The trend was reproducible. The significance of these result is that in an industrial separation of CO2/CH4, such as natural gas sweetening process, the plasticization effect of carbon dioxide will enhance permeation of methane and reduce the overall process selectivity.
texts.text: CH4 and N2 permeation experiments at 100 psig were repeated after the membrane was exposed to CO2 and O2, respectively. The repeat experiments were carried out for a period of 8 h for each gas. N2 permeance after exposure to O2 showed 5% higher value compared to that of before exposure to O2 with the value of 3.28 GPU compared with 3.14 GPU. The permeance of CH4 after CO2 was higher than that before CO2 at the beginning, but gradually declined and leveled off at a slightly lower value as shown in Fig. 3. This observation is explained by a fading plasticization effect of CO2, which allowed the polymer chains resume their original shape and rigidity. The trend was reproducible. The significance of these result is that in an industrial separation of CO2/CH4, such as natural gas sweetening process, the plasticization effect of carbon dioxide will enhance permeation of methane and reduce the overall process selectivity.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Gas mixture experiments
texts.text: 4.2. Gas mixture experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the discussion and interpretation of the gas mixture experimental results for CO2/CH4 separation through hollow fiber membranes, the following four factors should be considered [20,21]:
texts.text: For the discussion and interpretation of the gas mixture experimental results for CO2/CH4 separation through hollow fiber membranes, the following four factors should be considered [20,21]:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) Competitive sorption : Refers to competition of gases for limited Langmuir sorption sites and is present in almost all situations for glassy polymeric membranes. This effect tends to reduce the permeance of gases in the absence of plasticization. In the present study the competitive sorption effect plays an important role because of the high CO2 affinity to polymeric membranes, particularly to polyimide.
texts.text: Competitive sorption : Refers to competition of gases for limited Langmuir sorption sites and is present in almost all situations for glassy polymeric membranes. This effect tends to reduce the permeance of gases in the absence of plasticization. In the present study the competitive sorption effect plays an important role because of the high CO2 affinity to polymeric membranes, particularly to polyimide.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) Plasticization : The plasticization effect due to the interaction of CO2 gas with polymer accelerates the diffusivity of penetrants because of the swelling of the polymer matrix. Since the dense selective layer of a hollow fiber membrane is very thin, plasticization may occur at even very low feed CO2 partial pressures. As it is seen in Fig. 2, the increasing trend of CO2 permeance with pressure is due to this phenomenon.
texts.text: Plasticization : The plasticization effect due to the interaction of CO2 gas with polymer accelerates the diffusivity of penetrants because of the swelling of the polymer matrix. Since the dense selective layer of a hollow fiber membrane is very thin, plasticization may occur at even very low feed CO2 partial pressures. As it is seen in Fig. 2, the increasing trend of CO2 permeance with pressure is due to this phenomenon.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (3) Stage -cut and concentration polarization effects : At high stage-cuts a decrease in CO2 permeance and separation efficiency will occur which is due to the depletion of CO2 in the feed side and reduction in /Delta1 p CO2 . In addition, at high stage-cuts, especially in the case of membranes with high permeability and selectivity, concentration polarization will occur which tends to reduce separation efficiency.
texts.text: Stage -cut and concentration polarization effects : At high stage-cuts a decrease in CO2 permeance and separation efficiency will occur which is due to the depletion of CO2 in the feed side and reduction in /Delta1 p CO2 . In addition, at high stage-cuts, especially in the case of membranes with high permeability and selectivity, concentration polarization will occur which tends to reduce separation efficiency.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 6. CO2 permeance vs. stage-cut for various feed CO2 concentrations. CO2/CH4 separation using Cardo-type polyimide module; feed pressure, 100 psig.
texts.text: Fig. 6. CO2 permeance vs. stage-cut for various feed CO2 concentrations. CO2/CH4 separation using Cardo-type polyimide module; feed pressure, 100 psig.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (4) Non -ideal behavior of CO 2: The non-ideal gas behavior of CO2 which was not taken into consideration in this study because the pressures were not high.
texts.text: Non -ideal behavior of CO 2: The non-ideal gas behavior of CO2 which was not taken into consideration in this study because the pressures were not high.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.1. CO2/CH4 separation using Cardo-type polyimide (PI) membrane
texts.text: 4.2.1. CO2/CH4 separation using Cardo-type polyimide (PI) membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2/CH4 separation experiments were carried out in the following order of CO2 mole fraction in the feed: 0% (pure CH4), 5%, 10%, 24.6% (balance CH4) and 100% (pure CO2). The results are summarized in Figs. 6 and 7 and Table 2.
texts.text: The CO2/CH4 separation experiments were carried out in the following order of CO2 mole fraction in the feed: 0% (pure CH4), 5%, 10%, 24.6% (balance CH4) and 100% (pure CO2). The results are summarized in Figs. 6 and 7 and Table 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 6 shows the CO2 permeance as a function of stage-cut at different feed CO2 concentrations. While permeance is supposed to be independent of applied pressure (or partial pressure in a gas mixture), the results shown in Fig. 6 indicate that CO2 permeance at fixed stage-cuts (e.g. 5%) increased with its mole fraction (partial pressure) in the feed. This can be due to the combined effects of the above mentioned factors dominated by plasticization effect. This trend is similar to the one observed with the pure gas results as shown in Fig. 2, where CO2 permeance increased with pressure. In the case of mixed gas experiments the permeance increased with increasing partial pressure of CO2.
texts.text: Fig. 6 shows the CO2 permeance as a function of stage-cut at different feed CO2 concentrations. While permeance is supposed to be independent of applied pressure (or partial pressure in a gas mixture), the results shown in Fig. 6 indicate that CO2 permeance at fixed stage-cuts (e.g. 5%) increased with its mole fraction (partial pressure) in the feed. This can be due to the combined effects of the above mentioned factors dominated by plasticization effect. This trend is similar to the one observed with the pure gas results as shown in Fig. 2, where CO2 permeance increased with pressure. In the case of mixed gas experiments the permeance increased with increasing partial pressure of CO2.

texts.text: Fig. 7. CO2/CH4 permselectivity vs. stage-cut for various feed CO2 concentrations. Cardo-type polyimide module; feed pressure, 100 psig.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cardo-type polyimide module; feed pressure, 100 psig.
texts.text: Cardo-type polyimide module; feed pressure, 100 psig.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the competitive sorption of CO2 and CH4, the CH4 permeance is expected to decrease with increasing CO2 partial pressure, which is not necessarily supported by the experimental data. For example, at a fixed stage-cut of 5%, with an increase in the feed CO2 mole fraction from 0.05 to 0.246, CH4 permeance increased from 2.0 to 2.1 GPU. Although this increase was marginal, the same trend was consistently observed in other stage-cuts. Hence, the effects of the feed CO2 mole fraction on the permeances of both CO2 and CH4 can be explained by plasticization effect rather than competitive sorption. It was also observed that the decline of CO2 permeance with stage-cut was more significant than that of CH4. Based on the dual-mode sorption model presented by Koros et al. [21], the primary effect for the permeation of gases in a mixture is the competition between penetrants to occupy the fixed unrelaxed free volume in the polymer, which may cause significant depression in sorption of both gases in a binary mixture. In the case of CO2/CH4 mixtures, CO2 with a much larger affinity constant tends to exclude CH4 from Langmuir sorption sites.
texts.text: Based on the competitive sorption of CO2 and CH4, the CH4 permeance is expected to decrease with increasing CO2 partial pressure, which is not necessarily supported by the experimental data. For example, at a fixed stage-cut of 5%, with an increase in the feed CO2 mole fraction from 0.05 to 0.246, CH4 permeance increased from 2.0 to 2.1 GPU. Although this increase was marginal, the same trend was consistently observed in other stage-cuts. Hence, the effects of the feed CO2 mole fraction on the permeances of both CO2 and CH4 can be explained by plasticization effect rather than competitive sorption. It was also observed that the decline of CO2 permeance with stage-cut was more significant than that of CH4. Based on the dual-mode sorption model presented by Koros et al. [21], the primary effect for the permeation of gases in a mixture is the competition between penetrants to occupy the fixed unrelaxed free volume in the polymer, which may cause significant depression in sorption of both gases in a binary mixture. In the case of CO2/CH4 mixtures, CO2 with a much larger affinity constant tends to exclude CH4 from Langmuir sorption sites.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, two competitive and opposite effects governed the permeation of gases in the present study. On one hand, the greater affinity of CO2 towards the polymer blocked the available paths for the permeation of CH4 and on the other hand, as the partial pressure of CO2 decreased along the length of the hollow fibers CH4 found a better chance to penetrate through the dense layer and permeate to the other side. Thus, the CO2 permeance decreased and CH4 permeance increased along the length of the hollow fibers.
texts.text: In summary, two competitive and opposite effects governed the permeation of gases in the present study. On one hand, the greater affinity of CO2 towards the polymer blocked the available paths for the permeation of CH4 and on the other hand, as the partial pressure of CO2 decreased along the length of the hollow fibers CH4 found a better chance to penetrate through the dense layer and permeate to the other side. Thus, the CO2 permeance decreased and CH4 permeance increased along the length of the hollow fibers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall result was manifested in the form of a sharp decline in the CO2 permeance with stage-cut compared to that of CH4.
texts.text: The overall result was manifested in the form of a sharp decline in the CO2 permeance with stage-cut compared to that of CH4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition, it was noted that similar permeances were obtained at similar ratios of stage-cut to feed concentration. This was most obvious at a ratio of 1. That is, 5% stage-cut at 5% CO2 feed and 10% stage-cut at 10% CO2 feed, where the permeances were in the vicinity of 58 GPU.
texts.text: In addition, it was noted that similar permeances were obtained at similar ratios of stage-cut to feed concentration. This was most obvious at a ratio of 1. That is, 5% stage-cut at 5% CO2 feed and 10% stage-cut at 10% CO2 feed, where the permeances were in the vicinity of 58 GPU.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 7 shows the CO2/CH4 permselectivities at different feed compositions and stage-cuts. As can be seen in this figure, the CO2/CH4 permselectivity was close to ideality at lower stage-cuts. This trend was more obvious at CO2 mole fraction of 0.246. It should also be noted that both CO2 permeance and CO2/CH4 permselectivity at 0.246 CO2 mole fraction, as depicted in Figs. 6 and 7, were higher than their corresponding values obtained from the pure gas permeation experiments (the dashed lines in Figs. 6 and 7). These results corresponded to CO2 partial pressure difference ( /Delta1 p CO2 ) of 10-13.5 psi, which were far lower than 100 psi used for CO2 and CH4 pure gas experiments. This phenomenon was also observed by Kazama et al. [12] with CO2/N2 separation. They called this phenomenon 'mixed gas effect' or 'interaction effect', assuming that the effect was caused by competitive sorption of CO2 and N2 in the case of gas mixtures. The decrease in CO2/CH4 permselectivity with an increase in the stage-cut was due to the sharper decline of CO2 permeance compared to CH4 as was already discussed. Similarly, CO2/CH4 permselectivity declined with decreasing feed concentration of carbon dioxide at a fixed stage-cut.
texts.text: Fig. 7 shows the CO2/CH4 permselectivities at different feed compositions and stage-cuts. As can be seen in this figure, the CO2/CH4 permselectivity was close to ideality at lower stage-cuts. This trend was more obvious at CO2 mole fraction of 0.246. It should also be noted that both CO2 permeance and CO2/CH4 permselectivity at 0.246 CO2 mole fraction, as depicted in Figs. 6 and 7, were higher than their corresponding values obtained from the pure gas permeation experiments (the dashed lines in Figs. 6 and 7). These results corresponded to CO2 partial pressure difference ( /Delta1 p CO2 ) of 10-13.5 psi, which were far lower than 100 psi used for CO2 and CH4 pure gas experiments. This phenomenon was also observed by Kazama et al. [12] with CO2/N2 separation. They called this phenomenon 'mixed gas effect' or 'interaction effect', assuming that the effect was caused by competitive sorption of CO2 and N2 in the case of gas mixtures. The decrease in CO2/CH4 permselectivity with an increase in the stage-cut was due to the sharper decline of CO2 permeance compared to CH4 as was already discussed. Similarly, CO2/CH4 permselectivity declined with decreasing feed concentration of carbon dioxide at a fixed stage-cut.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 2 tabulates CO2 concentrations in the feed, retentate, andpermeatestreamsateachstage-cut.Theresultsindicatedthat the PI membrane performed the best at higher CO2 feed concentrations. For example the carbon dioxide content of 24.6% in feed was reduced to 8.1% when the stage-cut was set at 25%. CO2 concentration in the permeate, on the other hand, became 72.4%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While the discussions offered for PI membrane also apply to PPO, it is notable that the latter membrane showed higher permeances and lower separation factors compared to PI membrane. It should, however, be mentioned that in the case of PPO hollow fiber membranes, plasticization seemed to be the dominant factor in governing the permeation of the gases and the effect of competitive sorption was negligible. Another difference between PPO and PI was that the mixed gas effect was not observed for PPO, which can be explained by the fact that CO2 has less affinity to PPO compared to PI.
texts.text: While the discussions offered for PI membrane also apply to PPO, it is notable that the latter membrane showed higher permeances and lower separation factors compared to PI membrane. It should, however, be mentioned that in the case of PPO hollow fiber membranes, plasticization seemed to be the dominant factor in governing the permeation of the gases and the effect of competitive sorption was negligible. Another difference between PPO and PI was that the mixed gas effect was not observed for PPO, which can be explained by the fact that CO2 has less affinity to PPO compared to PI.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is also notable that, like in PI case, similar permeances were obtained at similar ratios of stage-cut to feed concentration. For example, at a ratio of 1 (5% stage-cut at 5% feed and 10% stagecut at 10% feed) the permeances were in the vicinity of 120 GPU independent of both feed concentration and stage-cut.
texts.text: It is also notable that, like in PI case, similar permeances were obtained at similar ratios of stage-cut to feed concentration. For example, at a ratio of 1 (5% stage-cut at 5% feed and 10% stagecut at 10% feed) the permeances were in the vicinity of 120 GPU independent of both feed concentration and stage-cut.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.4. Use of PI and PPO membranes in industrial settings
texts.text: 4.4. Use of PI and PPO membranes in industrial settings

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The characteristics of PI and PPO membranes bear a significant application in a cascade design combining these two membranes. In such design, the permeate from a PI module, which is rich in CO2 and lean in CH4, is fed into a PPO module for methane recovery. The retentate from PPO module, which is rich in methane, would be recycled to the intake of the PI module for further treatment. The high permeability and low selectivity of PPO membrane are the exact characteristics that are needed for low cost recovery of the lost methane through PI membrane stage. The value added final product from such cascade justifies the associated costs.
texts.text: The characteristics of PI and PPO membranes bear a significant application in a cascade design combining these two membranes. In such design, the permeate from a PI module, which is rich in CO2 and lean in CH4, is fed into a PPO module for methane recovery. The retentate from PPO module, which is rich in methane, would be recycled to the intake of the PI module for further treatment. The high permeability and low selectivity of PPO membrane are the exact characteristics that are needed for low cost recovery of the lost methane through PI membrane stage. The value added final product from such cascade justifies the associated costs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Also, the results emphasized the fact that membrane is an ideal tool for bulk removal of carbon dioxide from natural gas in large-scale industrial applications. A technically efficient and economically viable option for a large-scale application is a hybrid system consisting of a membrane system for bulk removal of carbon dioxide followed by a secondary process, such as amine absorption, for fine tuning. Such hybrid system would benefit from the bests of the two processes, i.e., a significantly lower load to the amine process elongates the regeneration cycle, and on the other hand, the high concentration of carbon dioxide in the feed enhances its selectivity causing a negligible loss of methane to the permeate (the reject stream in this case).
texts.text: Also, the results emphasized the fact that membrane is an ideal tool for bulk removal of carbon dioxide from natural gas in large-scale industrial applications. A technically efficient and economically viable option for a large-scale application is a hybrid system consisting of a membrane system for bulk removal of carbon dioxide followed by a secondary process, such as amine absorption, for fine tuning. Such hybrid system would benefit from the bests of the two processes, i.e., a significantly lower load to the amine process elongates the regeneration cycle, and on the other hand, the high concentration of carbon dioxide in the feed enhances its selectivity causing a negligible loss of methane to the permeate (the reject stream in this case).

texts.text: M. Pourafshari et al. / Separation and Purification Technology 51 (2006) 359-366

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The single gas permeation and gas mixture separation experiments indicated that Cardo-type polyimide hollow fiber membrane and polyphenylene oxide hollow fiber membrane had suitable characteristics for CO2/CH4 and O2/N2 separations. The former exhibited high permselectivity and moderate permeance towards the experimental gases, while the latter showed moderate permselectivity and high permeability towards the experimental gases.
texts.text: The single gas permeation and gas mixture separation experiments indicated that Cardo-type polyimide hollow fiber membrane and polyphenylene oxide hollow fiber membrane had suitable characteristics for CO2/CH4 and O2/N2 separations. The former exhibited high permselectivity and moderate permeance towards the experimental gases, while the latter showed moderate permselectivity and high permeability towards the experimental gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both membranes gave similar permeances at similar ratios of stage-cut to feed concentration, independent of both stage-cut and feed concentration.
texts.text: Both membranes gave similar permeances at similar ratios of stage-cut to feed concentration, independent of both stage-cut and feed concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Application of both membranes is advantageous in natural gas sweetening industry. A suggested process would be a hybrid systemconsisting of a membrane cascade containing PI and PPO membranes for the bulk removal of carbon dioxide followed by a secondary process, such as amine, for final cleaning of the gas stream. Finally, PPO showed a stable permeation rate with time while, the permeability of PI declined after 3 months of operation.
texts.text: Application of both membranes is advantageous in natural gas sweetening industry. A suggested process would be a hybrid systemconsisting of a membrane cascade containing PI and PPO membranes for the bulk removal of carbon dioxide followed by a secondary process, such as amine, for final cleaning of the gas stream. Finally, PPO showed a stable permeation rate with time while, the permeability of PI declined after 3 months of operation.
